symbols: Daniel with hands clasped in prayer — unwavering faith in God even in mortal peril
lions surrounding him peacefully — God shutting the mouths of the lions, divine mastery over nature
upward gaze — trust directed toward heaven, the source of deliverance
bones and skull on the ground — evidence that the lions are real killers, making Daniel's preservation miraculous
dark cave or pit — the place of death that becomes a place of testimony to God's power
red cloak — royal favor and the cost of faithfulness in a pagan court
description: A dramatic Baroque painting showing a young, muscular man with long dark hair seated on a rock inside a dark cave or pit, surrounded by approximately eight large lions. The man is bare-chested, wearing only a white loincloth and a red cloak draped behind him. His hands are clasped together in prayer and his face is turned upward with an expression of fervent supplication. The lions — massive, golden-maned beasts — surround him on all sides. Some lie calmly at his feet, others press close to him, one appears to nuzzle or lick his hand. Despite their power and proximity, none attack.
theological_description: Daniel in the Lions' Den is one of the most celebrated narratives of divine deliverance in the Old Testament. Daniel 6 records how the prophet, a high official in the Persian court of King Darius, continued to pray openly to God despite a royal decree forbidding prayer to anyone but the king. His enemies, unable to find fault in his governance, used his faithfulness against him. Cast into the den of lions, Daniel spent the night among the beasts. At dawn, King Darius rushed to the den and cried out, 'O Daniel, servant of the living God, has your God, whom you serve continually, been able to deliver you from the lions?' Daniel answered, 'My God sent his angel and shut the lions' mouths, and they have not harmed me' (Daniel 6:20-22). The bones on the ground heighten the miracle — these are man-eating lions, yet they became gentle as lambs before the man of God. Theologically, this scene is a powerful type of resurrection and vindication. Just as Daniel was sealed in the den and emerged alive, so Christ was sealed in the tomb and rose on the third day. Hebrews 11:33 lists those who 'through faith... stopped the mouths of lions' as exemplars of the faith that overcomes the world. Daniel's clasped hands and upward gaze embody the prayer of Psalm 91:13: 'You will tread on the lion and the cobra; you will trample the great lion and the serpent.

scripture_refs.reason: The narrative of Daniel cast into the lions' den and God shutting the lions' mouths is the direct event depicted
Daniel's practice of praying three times daily toward Jerusalem, the faithfulness that led to his arrest
The heroes of faith who through faith stopped the mouths of lions, with Daniel as the primary exemplar
The promise of treading on the lion, fulfilled in Daniel's miraculous preservation
The devil prowls like a roaring lion — Daniel's victory over literal lions prefigures the believer's victory over the spiritual adversary
